CONCLUS ION
The conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act require
Connissioner Jonas to disqualify himself frorn participating in
decisions regarding the proposed ordinance if it is reasonably
foreseeabl,e that the pending governmental decisions lrill 
have a
material financj.al effect on his econornic interests,
distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally, as
discussed below.
FACTS
Daniel M. Jonas is a commissioner on the Napa county
conservation, Development and Planning Connission. Comrnissioner
Jonas is president of, and holds one-third of the eguity in,
TripIe Delta Incorporated ("TripIe DeIta"), a closely-heId
corioration Iocated in the city of Napa, in Napa county. Triple
oelta regularly engages in the construction of residential and
connerciil projects, both contractually and speculatively.
Neither rriple- Delta nor cornrnissioner Jonas has ovrned or developed
any land in the unincorporated areas.of Napa county. during the
palt twelve months nor does either, in the foreseeable future,
ptan to purchase or develop any such land. Their future plans,
irowever,-could change should either be presented $rith a favorable
development opportunitY.
Commissioner Jonas' spouse, Janet E- Jonas, is currently
employed as a real estate agent for ColdlrelI Banker, Classical
erLpeities ("coldweIl Banke;") ' a real estate firm located in the
city of Napa, in Napa county. As a commissioned agent for
coldwelI Blnker, Ms. Jonas assists the firn in marketing its
Iisted properties including, fro:a time to tirne, existing hones
Located in unincorporated areas of Napa county. Ms. Jonas
currently has one iingle-family hone in escrow in an
unincorp6rated area oi ltapa county, but has earned no comnissions
in the past twelve nonths from the sale of property located in
that area.
The housing element of the Napa county general plan states
that the provision of affordable housing in the county is-to be
encouraged in connection with the developnent of residential
projecti. 
The Napa county Conservation, Developnent and Planning
bonmission is currently scheduled to consider whether to recomnend
to the Napa county Board of Supervisors that an inclusionary
housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part of a
cornprehensive response to this aspect of the county's general
plan. 
Such a recommendation, if raade, could include endorsement
of a model ordinance requiring sone or all of the following: 
(1)
that a certain percentage of aII new residential units constructed
in a project be traf fordablerr as that terrn is defined in the
ordinance,' (2) that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund
in lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or


our File No. I-91-555
Page 3
(3) that construction of a certain number of affordable units take
place off-site. 
In addition, the Napa county conservation,
bevelopnent and Planning Cornmission is likely to recommend the
creati-on of additional sources of funding for affordable housing
through means such as assessment of }inkage fees frorn developers
of agiicultural, 
commercial and industrial projects based upon
proj;ct-generated employment, iurposition of a business license
tax, or increase in documentary transfer or sales taxes- Such
taxes, if imposed or increased, Lrould be inapplicable to
Cornmissioner Jonas' business and property interests located within
the City of Napa. Should comnissioner Jonas or Triple Delta ever
decide Lo comnence a development project in unincorporated areas
of Napa county, such a project would be subject to any ordinance
ultirnately adopted by the Napa county Board of Supervisors.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials 
fron making,
participatinq in, oi using their official position to influence a
governninta I 
- decis ion in trhich they knold or have reason to knov'
€ney frave a financial interest. 
Cornmissioner Jonas is a public
official. 
(Section 82048. )
Financial Interests
An official 
has a financial interest in a decision within the
rneaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable . that the
aecisi6n wiII have a naterial financial effect, distinguishable
fron its effect on the public generally, on the official, 
or on a
rlru"i of the of f icial'i 
irnrnediate farnily,3 or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public-
official 
has a direct or indirect lnvestroent L'orth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more'
(b) Any real property in trhich the public
officiai 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
(c) AnY source of income, other than gifts
and other thin loans by a conmercj.al lending
institution 
j.n the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received-by or prornised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the tine when the
decision is made.
3  An official′ s ::immediate family:' are his or her spouse and
dependent children。  (SeCtion 82029。
)

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or hol,ds any position of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any interrnediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars (5250) or Inore in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official 
within 12 nonths prior to the time when
the decision is rnade.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest neans any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, 
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,
spouse, and dependent children own direct).y,
indirectly, or beneficially a lo-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103.
Commissioner Jonas has a number of potentially disqualifying
economic i.nterests vrhich must be evaluated to deternine whether he
must disqualify himself from participating in governmental
decisioni regalding the proposed ordinance. He has an economic
interest in iriple Delta as president and holder of one third of
the equity of this closely-heId corporation engaged in the.
constiuction of residentiat and connercial projects. 
Comrnissioner
Jonas' econonic interests include a pro-rata share of the income
of Triple De1ta. (Section 82030.) In addition, cli'ents of Triple
Delta are also considered sources of income to Commissioner Jonas'
(RusselI Advice Letter, No' A-88-484. )
Mr. Jonas' economic interests also include his cornrnunity
property interest in the incorne of his spouse. (section.82030' )
iou'navl advised us that l'ts. Jonas is a real estate agent engaged
in the sale of real estate within the jurisdiction of NaPa county'
Regulation L8704.3 provj,des that the followinq are deemed to be
so,irc"= of income to a real estate agent within the meaning of
subdivision (c) of Section 87103:
(A) The broker and brokerage business entity
under v/hose auspices the agent $rorks;
(B) The person the agent rePresents in the
transaction; and


Our File No. f-91-555
Page 5
(c) Any person who receives a finder's or
other referral fee for referring a party to the
transaction to the broker, or who nakes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.
Regulation 18704.3 (c) (3) .
Accordingly, because Comnrissioner Jonas has a cornmunity
property interest in the incone of his spouse, we rnust consider
the naterial effect of the pending decisions on the following
economic interests of his spouse: (1) cold$re]f Banker which is
the broker and brokerage business where Ms. Jonas works as a
commissioned real estate agent,' (2) Any person who has been a
source of cornmission income to l'ts. Jonas of $500 or rnore in the
preceding 12 nonthsi and (3) Any person who has received a fee for
ieferring a party to a transaction to coIdvrell Banker.
consequently, Comrnissioner Jonas nust consider the foreseeable and
material financial effect of decisions regarding the proposed
ordinance on each of these econonic interests to deternine whether
he must disqualify himself from participating in such decisions'
Makinq, Participatinq in Making. or Attenptinq to Influence a
Governmental Decis ion
A public official 
makes a governnental decision or
participates in the naking of a governnental decision whenever the
public officiat 
votes on a natter, cornmits the agency to a course
ot action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).) AdditionaIly, a public
official 
participates in a governnental decision when, acting
within thl authoiity of his or her position, the public official:
(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governnental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2) Advises or makes recornmendat ions to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) conducting research or naking any
investigation which requj.res the exercise of
judgrment on the part of the official 
or
designated employee and the purpose of lrhich
is to influence the decision; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the


Our File NO. I-9■
-556
Page 6
part of the official or designated enployee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 18700 (c) .
with regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's 
agency or an agency apPointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official 
is
attempting to use his or her official 
position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official 
contacts, or appears before, or otherrrise attempts to
i,nf luence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attenpts to influence include, but are not limited to,
uipeaiun..= or contacts by the official 
on behalf of a business
enlity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
economic inteiests of Commissioner Jonas vill 
be materially
affected by a governrnental decision, he rnust not only disqualify
hiraself fron participating in fornal decisions of the Napa County
conservation,- Development and Planning connission which nay affect
such interests, but he must also abstain fro:n attempting to
influence such decisions by communicating with other menbers of
the conmission or the staff regarding the decisions.
Foreseeabi I itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likel.ihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; 
however, certainty is not required. 
(Do!,nev Cares v'
-Downev Cornmunitv Development Comm' (1987) 196 cal'App.3d 983, 989-
,rt- 
". U9II9y Qs77) 70 cal.App.3d 817; rn re Thorner (1975)
r ri,rc ops, 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conf lict-s of interest; it seeks to Prevent even the appearance of
a possibLe conf lict of interest. 
(witt v. U.9.f.f-911, suDra at 823 ' )
During the course of our telephone conversation of
Decenber 26, L9gI, Mr. Jonas directed my attention to In re
Thorner, supra, where the commission discussed the concept of
foreseeaUifity within several hypothetical situations. 
rn
Thorner, the Cornnission deternined foreseeability within-the
c"ntext of applicants in which a public official 
had a financial
interest applaring before the official's 
agency. The facts in
your reque-st for idvice do not involve applicants appearing before
the agency.


our File No. r-91-555
Page 7
Thorner, however, also discusses disqual ification in a
context which is sinilar to the facts in your request for advice
and concludes that it is foreseeable that the financial interests
of the public officiaL will be rnaterially affected by the pending
decisions in a rnanner which is distinguishable from the effect of
the decisions on the public Aenerally. In Thorner, the pending
decision related to lifting 
a building noratoriun. The commission
concluded that although the proposed decisions night result in
financial benefit to a large segTnent of the conmunity, the public
official's 
business was in a position to real,ize irnnediate,
substantial and specific financial gains as a result of renewed
building activity if the building noratorium were lifted.
accordinqly, the effect of the pending decisions on the public
official 
wEre distinguishable fron the effect on the public
generally and the official 
was disqualified fron participating in
the decisions. (Thorner, EUP-!.a, at p. 208. )
Sirnilarly, it is foreseeable that the proPosed ordinance will
have an effect on Cornrnissioner Jonas' econoni'c interests. 
The
ordinance pertains to the developrnent of affordable housing in the
county of Napa where both Triple Delta and Commissioner Jonas'
spousL do buliness. Requiring that developers either include
aifordable housing in their projects or that they pay fees into a
fund to provide aifordable housing is )'ikeIy-to affect the profits
for tripie Delta, a business entity engaged in the construction of
residential and conmercial property. Also, as the cost of
developing residential and comrnercial property in the
unincolpoiated areas of the county increases, there is substantial
likelihood that comnercial and residential devel-opment in the city
wiII be affected.
It is also foreseeable that !trs. Jonas' source of incone,
coldvelI Banker, wil.I be affected by the proposed ordinance.
Affordable housing prices tend to be lower thus generating lo$'er
sales commissions for the real estate businesses who rePresent
buyers and sellers in transactions involving such hornes.
l.toieover, I'Irs. Jonas, connission income will be affected sinilarly
by the proposed ordinance. on the other hand, more affordable
housing-ma| result in increased sales and, consequently, increased
comnissions. Accordingly, the foreseeabiiity el6rnent is met.{ 
i.Ie
now deterrnine lvhether the effect of the pending decisions on
comrnissioner Jonas' econornic interests wiIl be naterial.
4 During the course of our telephone conversation of December 26 1
1991, colnmissioner Jonas advised ne that Triple Delta had not done

we have insufficient

inctudes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or

If any of the sources of incorne or promised incone mentioned

The effect of a decision is naterial as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official 
if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision r.rill affect the individual's
incone, investrnents, or other tangible or
intangible assets or Iiabilities 
(other than real
proPerty) by $r,000 or more; or

s
real property interest in a manner that is
cons■dered mater■al under Section 
■8702.3 or
section ■8702.4.
Regulation 187 02 .66 .
Please note, holrever, that if the source of income is a

PubIic cenera I lv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision is rnaterial, disqua I if icat ion is reguired
only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally. 
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not
affect a1I nembers of the public in the same nanner,
disqualification nay be required unless the effect of the decision
on the public official's 
econo:nic interests is the sa:ne as the
effect on a significant segrnent of the public. 
(Regulation
18703.) For purposes of our discussion, the public is the entire
population of the county. (In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In
re owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77; Joroensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-
017 . )

We conclude that the rrpublic generalLy" exception is
inapplicable to commissioner Jonas. This is so because his
economic interests are peculiarly tied in with real estate
construction and sales, both areas which will be singularly

affordable housing in Napa county.

A public official

ot action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of

Foreseeabi I itv
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likel.ihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; 
however, certainty is not required.

FACTS Daniel M. Jonas is a commissioner on the Napa county conservation, Development and Planning Connission. Comrnissioner Jonas is president of, and holds one-third of the eguity in, TripIe Delta Incorporated ("TripIe DeIta"), a closely-heId corioration Iocated in the city of Napa, in Napa county. Their future plans, irowever,-could change should either be presented $rith a favorable development opportunitY. Commissioner Jonas' spouse, Janet E- Jonas, is currently employed as a real estate agent for ColdlrelI Banker, Classical erLpeities ("coldweIl Banke;") ' a real estate firm located in the city of Napa, in Napa county.
